title: Rollback first design
description: Plan rollback, disablement, or forward-fix boundaries before changing production-impacting code or state.
body: Use for release, migration, config, feature flag, or infrastructure changes that may need reversal. Do not claim rollback exists because git can revert source code; data and external state may not revert. Actions: name the rollback mechanism, owner, time window, data compatibility constraints, and validation signal after rollback. Validation signals include documented rollback command or procedure, smoke checks, and compatibility tests. Risk boundary: no credible rollback for a high-impact change requires human approval and staged rollout. Use when Production Change Risk Controls work touches repository conventions, toolchain files, changed symbols, validation commands, and runtime output. Do not use this guidance to override a more specific pack, user instruction, or repository contract. Actions: choose the smallest direction that preserves the domain contract; inspect the current files first, keep the change scoped to the owning boundary, and record what evidence changed the decision. Validation signals include package-local checks, focused tests, build output, smoke runs, and command logs. Risk boundary: escalate or stop when the task involves broad rewrites, generated-file drift, stale assumptions, and unvalidated behavior changes, missing owner evidence, or live external state.
